concepts.title: Name the compound, not just a formula

questions.text: Giving the chemical formula of a compound is enough when a question asks you to identify it.
questions.explanation: In 2022, students who gave a formula without a written name were not awarded the mark.
questions.studyTip: Asked to name/identify? Write the name in words.

questions.text: When asked to identify a compound, you must give its written name, not only a formula.
questions.explanation: Examiners require the name as well as, or instead of, a formula.

questions.text: As long as the number is right, the units on a chemistry answer do not matter.
questions.explanation: Across years, missing or incorrect units (e.g. kJ g per mol slips) were among the most common errors.
questions.studyTip: Every quantity needs the right unit.

questions.text: Answers must carry correct units, and quantities often need converting (e.g. mL to L, m3 to L).
questions.explanation: A wrong or missing unit costs the mark even with the right figure.
questions.studyTip: Check and convert units before and after calculating.

questions.text: Repeating a term already given in the question stem (e.g. 'incomplete combustion') counts as an explanation.
questions.explanation: In 2023, 'incomplete combustion' (already in the stem) was not accepted as the reason.

questions.text: You must explain beyond the stem; a term already provided in the question cannot be your answer.
questions.explanation: Marks reward added reasoning, not repetition of the prompt.
questions.studyTip: If it's in the question, it isn't your answer.

questions.text: For a multi-mark calculation, the final numerical answer alone is sufficient.
questions.explanation: In 2025, many did not complete a full ICE table; method marks need shown working.

questions.text: Show each step of a calculation; equilibrium problems usually need a full ICE table.
questions.explanation: Method marks depend on the working being visible and logical.
questions.studyTip: Use an ICE table for equilibrium calculations.

concepts.title: Energy released is a positive magnitude

questions.text: When stating the energy released by a reaction, you should include the negative sign from delta H.
questions.explanation: In 2025, including the negative sign while saying 'energy released' was a common error.

questions.text: Delta H is negative for an exothermic reaction, but the energy released is stated as a positive magnitude.
questions.explanation: The sign of delta H shows direction; the amount released is a positive number.
questions.studyTip: Sign shows direction; magnitude is positive.

questions.text: If a question uses an unfamiliar context, the underlying chemistry must be different from what you have learned.
questions.explanation: In 2022, novel contexts caused difficulty, though the chemistry was familiar.

questions.text: Novel contexts still test familiar principles; work through the information and apply what you know.
questions.explanation: Examiners design unfamiliar scenarios to test understanding, not new content.
questions.studyTip: Identify the principle, then apply it to the scenario.

questions.text: A gas volume in millilitres can be used directly in n = V / Vm without converting.
questions.explanation: In 2024, failing to divide a gas volume by 1000 to get litres was a common error.

questions.text: Convert volumes to the correct unit (mL to L by dividing by 1000, m3 to L) before using molar volume.
questions.explanation: Molar volume is per litre, so volumes must be in litres.

concepts.title: Use the change, not the final reading

questions.text: The volume of gas produced is simply the final volume reading.
questions.explanation: In 2024, using the final volume rather than (final minus initial) was a common error.

questions.text: The volume produced is the final reading minus the initial reading.
questions.explanation: Always take the difference for a 'produced' or 'change in' quantity.

concepts.title: Energy per gram vs per mole

questions.text: A fuel with a longer carbon chain always releases more energy per gram.
questions.explanation: In 2022, claiming octane delivers more energy per gram because of its longer chain was incorrect.
questions.studyTip: Per mole differs from per gram.

questions.text: A longer-chain fuel releases more energy per mole, but energy per gram must be calculated, not assumed.
questions.explanation: Divide molar energy by molar mass to compare per gram.
questions.studyTip: Per gram = per mole / molar mass.

concepts.title: Don't explain energy content by bond enthalpies

questions.text: Differences in the energy content of two fuels are explained by their different bond enthalpies.
questions.explanation: In 2025, citing bond enthalpies (rather than calculating) was not accepted.

questions.text: Energy content differences should be shown by full calculations from the combustion equations.
questions.explanation: Detailed bond-energy reasoning is beyond the study design here.
questions.studyTip: Use the thermochemical equations, not bond enthalpies.

concepts.title: Get the mole ratio from the balanced equation

questions.text: In a redox titration you can relate the two reactants directly without using the balanced equations.
questions.explanation: In 2024, students did not apply the second half-equation to establish the stoichiometry.

questions.text: Use the balanced (half-)equations to find the mole ratio before calculating quantities.
questions.explanation: The coefficients give the ratio needed for the calculation.
questions.studyTip: Balance, find the ratio, then calculate moles.

questions.text: Percentage yield and percentage atom economy measure the same thing.
questions.explanation: In 2024, some calculated only one when both were required; they are distinct.

questions.text: Percentage yield compares actual to theoretical product; atom economy compares useful product mass to total reactant mass.
questions.explanation: Yield is about how much you get; atom economy is about how little is wasted.
questions.studyTip: Yield = amount obtained; atom economy = efficiency.

questions.text: You can assume both reactants are fully consumed when calculating the amount of product.
questions.explanation: In 2025, some did not establish the quantity of excess reagent remaining after reaction.

questions.text: Determine the limiting reagent (and any excess remaining) before calculating product amounts.
questions.explanation: The reaction stops when the limiting reagent runs out.
questions.studyTip: Limiting reagent sets the maximum product.

questions.text: With an accurately calibrated calorimeter, energy released is found from a simple mass times specific heat of water approach.
questions.explanation: In 2022, the calibrated calorimeter meant water-only approaches were not acceptable.

questions.text: With a calibrated calorimeter, energy is found from the calibration factor: energy = CF times the temperature change.
questions.explanation: The calibration factor accounts for the whole apparatus, not just the water.

questions.text: Reduction is the loss of electrons, which increases the oxidation number.
questions.explanation: In 2025, incorrect terminology for electron transfer and oxidation numbers was common.

questions.text: Reduction is the gain of electrons (oxidation number decreases); oxidation is the loss of electrons.
questions.explanation: Track electrons and oxidation numbers carefully in redox.
questions.studyTip: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain.

questions.text: In a galvanic cell the negative electrode (anode) is where reduction occurs.
questions.explanation: In 2024, the fuel-cell question required recognising the negative electrode as the anode where oxidation occurs.

questions.text: Oxidation always occurs at the anode (the negative electrode in a galvanic cell).
questions.explanation: This holds for both galvanic and electrolytic cells.

questions.text: Half-equations are balanced the same way in acidic and basic conditions.
questions.explanation: In 2024, balancing half-equations in alkaline media was not well understood.

questions.text: In basic conditions you balance using H2O and OH-, not H+ as you would in acid.
questions.explanation: Add OH- to both sides to neutralise H+ when working in base.
questions.studyTip: Acidic: H+ and H2O. Basic: OH- and H2O.

questions.text: You can predict whether a redox reaction occurs without referring to the electrochemical series.
questions.explanation: In 2023, students did not reference or justify with the electrochemical series as the stem required.

questions.text: Predictions about redox reactions should be justified using the electrochemical series.
questions.explanation: Compare the relative strengths of oxidants and reductants from the series.
questions.studyTip: Stronger oxidant (higher) reacts with stronger reductant (lower).

questions.text: In electrolytic purification of a metal, the impure metal is connected as the cathode.
questions.explanation: In 2024, the practical aspects of purifying a metal by electrowinning were not well understood.
questions.studyTip: Impure metal dissolves at the anode.

questions.text: In electrorefining, the impure metal is the anode (it dissolves) and pure metal deposits at the cathode.
questions.explanation: Oxidation at the anode dissolves the impure metal; reduction deposits pure metal.
questions.studyTip: Impure anode dissolves; pure metal plates the cathode.

questions.text: A thermochemical equation only needs the delta H value, not the physical states of the substances.
questions.explanation: In 2025, failing to supply the correct states with an enthalpy value was a common error.

questions.text: Thermochemical equations must show the correct states, because delta H depends on them.
questions.explanation: The energy change differs if water is liquid or gas, for example.

questions.text: An exothermic reaction has a positive delta H because it releases energy.
questions.explanation: Sign conventions for delta H were a recurring source of error.

questions.text: An exothermic reaction has a negative delta H, releasing energy to the surroundings.
questions.explanation: Endothermic reactions absorb energy and have a positive delta H.

questions.text: A thermometer resting on the bottom of the calorimeter can will still give a reliable temperature.
questions.explanation: In 2023, a good response identified the thermometer resting on the bottom as a source of error.

questions.text: A thermometer touching the metal can reads too high; it should sit in the liquid for a valid reading.
questions.explanation: Contact with the heated metal biases the reading upward.
questions.studyTip: Suspend the thermometer in the liquid.

questions.text: Both galvanic and electrolytic cells convert electrical energy into chemical energy.
questions.explanation: Distinguishing the energy-conversion direction of the two cell types is essential.

questions.text: A galvanic cell converts chemical energy to electrical (spontaneous); an electrolytic cell uses electrical energy to drive a non-spontaneous reaction.
questions.explanation: Galvanic cells supply current; electrolytic cells consume it.
questions.studyTip: Galvanic makes electricity; electrolytic uses it.

questions.text: In reaching equilibrium only the reactant concentrations change; products stay constant.
questions.explanation: In 2022, students failed to indicate that both reactants and products changed in concentration.

questions.text: Reaching equilibrium involves changes in both reactant and product concentrations until the rates are equal.
questions.explanation: Equilibrium is dynamic: forward and reverse rates become equal, not zero.
questions.studyTip: Constant concentrations, equal rates - not no reaction.

questions.text: Adding an inert gas to an equilibrium at constant volume shifts it toward the side with fewer gas molecules.
questions.explanation: In 2025, students wrongly claimed an inert gas causes a forward shift.
questions.studyTip: Inert gas at constant V = no shift.

questions.text: Adding an inert gas at constant volume raises total pressure but does not change partial pressures, so the equilibrium does not shift.
questions.explanation: Only changes to the partial pressures of reacting species shift the equilibrium.
questions.studyTip: No change to partial pressures = no shift.

concepts.title: Le Chatelier - link to the stress

questions.text: Saying 'the system shifts to oppose the change' fully answers a Le Chatelier question.
questions.explanation: In 2024, responses did not link specifically to the stress they had selected.

questions.text: You must link the response to the specific stress applied: which way it shifts and why for that exact change.
questions.explanation: Name the change, the direction of shift, and the reason.
questions.studyTip: State the stress, the direction, and the reason.

questions.text: A temperature change can explain an equilibrium shift even when the question states constant temperature.
questions.explanation: In 2024, temperature-based answers were rejected because the process was at constant temperature.

questions.text: If the question specifies constant temperature, the explanation cannot rely on a temperature change.
questions.explanation: Use the stress that actually changed, not one ruled out by the conditions.
questions.studyTip: Constant T rules out a temperature argument.

questions.text: A catalyst increases the yield at equilibrium by shifting the position of equilibrium.
questions.explanation: A catalyst does not change the equilibrium position or the value of K.

questions.text: A catalyst speeds up attainment of equilibrium but does not change the equilibrium position or yield.
questions.explanation: It lowers the activation energy for forward and reverse reactions equally.

questions.text: On a Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution, most particles have very high kinetic energy.
questions.explanation: In 2022, a good response described the peak as the most common KE with numbers decreasing either side.
questions.studyTip: The peak is the most common KE.

questions.text: The distribution peaks at the most common kinetic energy, with fewer particles at higher and lower energies.
questions.explanation: Only particles above the activation energy (the tail) can react.
questions.studyTip: Few particles reach the activation energy.

questions.text: Increasing the concentration of a reactant has no effect on the reaction rate.
questions.explanation: In 2022, the rate of oxidation should increase as the ethanol concentration increased.

questions.text: Increasing concentration raises the collision frequency, increasing the reaction rate.
questions.explanation: More frequent successful collisions speed up the reaction.
questions.studyTip: More particles per volume = more collisions.

questions.text: The equilibrium constant K changes when you change concentrations or add a catalyst.
questions.explanation: K is changed only by temperature, not by concentration changes or catalysts.

questions.text: The value of K depends only on temperature; concentration changes and catalysts do not alter it.
questions.explanation: Adding reactant shifts position but K is unchanged at constant temperature.
questions.studyTip: K is fixed at a given temperature.

questions.text: Cyclohexane and benzene have the same structure, both with alternating double bonds in the ring.
questions.explanation: In 2024, students confused the structural differences between cyclohexane and benzene.

questions.text: Cyclohexane is a saturated ring (single bonds); benzene has a delocalised ring of electrons, not localised double bonds.
questions.explanation: Benzene's delocalisation makes it more stable and behave differently.

questions.text: An ester formed in the presence of water is stable and will not react further.
questions.explanation: In 2024, water present means an ester can hydrolyse back rather than remaining as biodiesel.

questions.text: In the presence of water an ester can hydrolyse back to the acid and alcohol, reducing the biodiesel yield.
questions.explanation: Hydrolysis is the reverse of esterification.

questions.text: Saying 'oleic acid has one C=C double bond' fully distinguishes it from stearic acid.
questions.explanation: In 2024, it was insufficient to describe oleic without stating that stearic has no C=C bonds.

questions.text: You must state that stearic acid has no C=C bonds (is saturated), not just describe oleic acid.
questions.explanation: A comparison requires the relevant feature of each molecule.

questions.text: Structural isomers of a compound have different molecular formulas.
questions.explanation: In 2025, isomers share the molecular formula and molar mass; only the structure differs.

questions.text: Structural isomers have the same molecular formula and molar mass but a different arrangement of atoms.
questions.explanation: Their differing structures give different properties.

questions.text: Amine molecules experience only dispersion forces between them.
questions.explanation: In 2025, stating amines have only dispersion forces was a common error.

questions.text: Amines contain N-H bonds, so they can hydrogen bond, giving stronger forces than dispersion alone.
questions.explanation: Hydrogen bonding raises boiling points relative to non-polar molecules of similar size.
questions.studyTip: N-H or O-H present -> hydrogen bonding.

concepts.title: Oxidation pathway of a primary alcohol

questions.text: A primary alcohol oxidises directly to a carboxylic acid with no intermediate.
questions.explanation: Reaction pathways for functional groups are commonly examined.

questions.text: A primary alcohol oxidises to an aldehyde and then to a carboxylic acid.
questions.explanation: Controlled conditions can stop oxidation at the aldehyde.

concepts.title: Hydrolysis is not reversed by simple condensation

questions.text: Hydrolysed collagen (gelatin) can simply undergo a condensation reaction to reform the original collagen.
questions.explanation: In 2022, claiming gelatin can just condense to reform collagen was a common mistake.
questions.studyTip: Structure is not restored so simply.

questions.text: Once a protein is hydrolysed, its original structure is not restored by a simple condensation reaction.
questions.explanation: The specific sequence and folding are lost on hydrolysis.
questions.studyTip: Breaking is easy; rebuilding the exact structure is not.

questions.text: Esterification goes to completion, converting all the acid and alcohol to ester.
questions.explanation: Esterification reaches equilibrium rather than going to completion.

questions.text: Esterification is a reversible reaction that reaches equilibrium, catalysed by concentrated sulfuric acid.
questions.explanation: Removing water can drive the equilibrium toward the ester.
questions.studyTip: Acid + alcohol in equilibrium with ester + water.

questions.text: A condensed formula is always enough to identify a specific isomer.
questions.explanation: Condensed formulas can be ambiguous between isomers.

questions.text: You may need a structural formula to distinguish isomers that share a condensed formula.
questions.explanation: The arrangement of atoms, not just the count, defines the isomer.
questions.studyTip: Draw it out when isomers differ in arrangement.

questions.text: A compound can be identified as a carboxylic acid even without a broad O-H peak or a carbonyl peak in its IR spectrum.
questions.explanation: In 2022 and 2025, the absence of the broad acid O-H (and carbonyl) peak meant it could not be a carboxylic acid.
questions.studyTip: No broad O-H + C=O -> not an acid.

questions.text: A carboxylic acid shows a broad O-H peak (about 2500 to 3500 per cm) and a carbonyl peak; their absence rules it out.
questions.explanation: Use the presence or absence of characteristic peaks to identify functional groups.

questions.text: The base peak in a mass spectrum gives the relative molecular mass of the compound.
questions.explanation: In 2022, students discussed base peak or fragments instead of how the spectrum gives the relative molecular mass.

questions.text: The molecular ion peak (highest m/z), not the base peak, indicates the relative molecular mass.
questions.explanation: The base peak is the most intense fragment, not necessarily the whole molecule.
questions.studyTip: Highest m/z = molecular ion = relative molecular mass.

questions.text: Exact NMR chemical shift values can be used to pin down a specific structural feature.
questions.explanation: In 2025, using chemical shifts to justify a specific structure was not awarded marks.
questions.studyTip: Shifts give environment, not exact structure.

questions.text: Chemical shifts indicate the chemical environment of atoms but are not precise enough to justify a structure alone.
questions.explanation: Combine shift ranges with peak number, splitting and other data.
questions.studyTip: Use shift ranges with the rest of the evidence.

concepts.title: Link the feature to the data

questions.text: Stating a structural feature is enough; you need not point to the spectral data that shows it.
questions.explanation: In 2025, responses that did not relate data from spectra or the table to the feature lost marks.

questions.text: You must link each structural feature back to the specific spectral or table data that supports it.
questions.explanation: Identifications must be justified from the data provided.
questions.studyTip: Feature plus the peak or value that proves it.

questions.text: In a permanganate-oxalate titration you relate moles of permanganate to oxalate in a 1:1 ratio.
questions.explanation: In 2024, students did not apply the correct stoichiometry from the balanced equation.

questions.text: Use the balanced redox equation: MnO4- and C2O4 2- react in a 2:5 ratio, not 1:1.
questions.explanation: The coefficients from the combined half-equations give the ratio.

questions.text: The endpoint of a permanganate titration is best described as pink because the Mn2+ ion is pink.
questions.explanation: In 2025, 'pink' (citing Mn2+) was not accepted, as Mn2+ forms steadily throughout the titration.

questions.text: The endpoint is the first permanent purple colour from a slight excess of MnO4-.
questions.explanation: Up to the endpoint the purple is decolourised; the first lasting colour marks the end.

questions.text: Hydrolysis of lactose produces two glucose molecules.
questions.explanation: In 2023, students wrongly said lactose hydrolyses to two glucose units.

questions.text: Hydrolysis of lactose produces glucose and galactose.
questions.explanation: Different disaccharides give different monosaccharide products.
questions.studyTip: Lactose -> glucose + galactose; sucrose -> glucose + fructose.

questions.text: Changing pH has no effect on the structure or function of a protein.
questions.explanation: In 2022, the effect of pH on proteins was not well understood.

questions.text: A change in pH disrupts bonds in a protein's structure, causing denaturation and loss of function.
questions.explanation: Altered charges break interactions that maintain the tertiary structure.
questions.studyTip: pH change -> denaturation -> loss of function.

questions.text: When an amino acid such as proline is named in an enzyme, it is acting as the substrate.
questions.explanation: In 2023, students referred to proline as the substrate rather than part of the primary sequence.

questions.text: Such an amino acid is part of the enzyme's primary sequence, not the substrate it acts on.
questions.explanation: The substrate is the molecule the enzyme acts upon, distinct from the enzyme's own residues.
questions.studyTip: Enzyme residues build the enzyme, not the substrate.

questions.text: Stating that vitamin C 'is an antioxidant' explains how it protects another substance from oxidation.
questions.explanation: In 2022, just stating vitamin C is an antioxidant did not address the 'how' in the question.

questions.text: You must explain how it works: the antioxidant is preferentially oxidised, sparing the other substance.
questions.explanation: It reacts with the oxidiser first, protecting the target molecule.
questions.studyTip: Antioxidant is oxidised first, protecting the rest.
